text: The video shows a laparoscopic sigmoid resection for mild diverticulitis. A close-up view displays the EEA stapler with the handle on the right and the anvil and staple cartridge on the left, separated. The anvil is positioned in the proximal bowel end, while the green unit containing staples and a circular cutting knife is inserted into the rectal stump and advanced to the transection site. When the units are joined with overlapped bowel, the stapler is fired, placing two circular rows of staples and cutting out a circular donut to create an open lumen.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon upward and out of the pelvis, opening the left lateral attachment first. The left ureter is identified and preserved, followed by similar dissection on the right side. The bowel is encircled posteriorly, and dissection continues into the pelvis. Two applications of the transverse stapler are used to close and transect the upper rectum.
A small abdominal incision is made in the lower midline, and the sigmoid colon is delivered onto the abdominal wall. The surgeon divides the upper sigmoid colon using a purse string stapler.

The surgeon places the anvil of the EEA stapler into the proximal bowel, threading the shaft through the suture line. The resected specimen is removed, and the proximal bowel is returned into the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon identifies the rectal stump by its staple line. The EEA stapler is inserted through the rectum and advanced to the rectal stump, distending the staple line. The shaft is opened and advanced through the staple line. The anvil in the proximal bowel is passed over the shaft and secured. The EEA unit is closed, approximating the bowel ends, and fired, placing two circular rows of staples and cutting with the circular blade.

The surgeon fills the pelvis with saline solution and insufflates the rectum with air to check for leaks, observing for bubbles as an indicator. No leaks are detected. The surgical instruments are then removed, and the trocar sites are closed, marking the completion of the procedure.
This production was made possible by the generosity of Teresa and Bernard von der Schmidt.
